0: Environmental impact of primary beef production chain in Colombia: Carbon footprint, non-renewable energy and land use using Life Cycle Assessment
Ricardo González-Quintero a,* Diana María Bolívar-Vergara ¹ b Ngonidzashe Chirinda a, , Jacobo Arango a , Heiber Pantevez Rolando Barahona-Rosales b , María Solange Sánchez-Pinzón d

b Facultad de Ciencias Agrarias, Universidad Nacional de Colombia sede Medellín, Colombia

1:
<list>Most of GHG emissions in cow-calf and fattening farms arise from animal on- farm activities. 
Allocation methods for managing co- products influence the results of envi- ronmental impacts. 
Cow-calf and fattening farms were in a cluster with better environmental per- formance. 
Inputs used and meat yield determine the outcome of the carbon footprint. 
Better quality of animal feed can reduce the negative environmental burdens.</list>
3: Received 14 October 2020 Received in revised form 27 January 2021 Accepted 28 January 2021 Available online 4 February 2021
4: Climate change Colombian cattle systems Global warming potential Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions Livestock production systems Mitigation potential
5: In Colombia, the beef production chain accounts for approximately 11.6 million cattle heads and annually pro- duces 933 million kg of the beef carcass. There are no life cycle assessment (LCA) studies that have evaluated the environmental performance of Colombian beef systems. The present study aimed to estimate the carbon foot- print (CF), non-renewable energy use, and land use of 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms in Colombia. The study also aimed to identify the main hotspots of adverse environmental impacts and propose possible mitiga- tion options and their cost-effectiveness. The impact categories were estimated using the 2006 IPCC and the 2019 Refinement to 2006 IPCC guidelines, databases, and locally estimated emission factors. The functional units used were 1 kg fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) and 1 kg live weight gain (LWG), leaving the farm gate. Three methods of allocating environmental burdens to meat and milk products were applied: economic, en- ergy, and mass allocation. The adoption of improved pastures was considered a mitigation measure, and an eco- nomic assessment was performed to estimate the relative cost-effectiveness of its establishment. A principal component multivariate analysis and a Hierarchical Clustering on Principal Components were performed. The economic allocation method assigned a greater environmental burden to meat (83%), followed by energy content (80%) and mass production (73%). The largest sources of GHG emissions were enteric fermentation and manure deposited on pasture. Both cow-calf and fattening systems had a cluster of farms with better productivity, pasture

There are no life cycle assessment (LCA) studies that have evaluated the environmental performance of Colombian beef systems. The present study aimed to estimate the carbon foot- print (CF), non-renewable energy use, and land use of 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms in Colombia. The study also aimed to identify the main hotspots of adverse environmental impacts and propose possible mitiga- tion options and their cost-effectiveness. The impact categories were estimated using the 2006 IPCC and the 2019 Refinement to 2006 IPCC guidelines, databases, and locally estimated emission factors. The functional units used were 1 kg fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) and 1 kg live weight gain (LWG), leaving the farm gate. Three methods of allocating environmental burdens to meat and milk products were applied: economic, en- ergy, and mass allocation. The adoption of improved pastures was considered a mitigation measure, and an eco- nomic assessment was performed to estimate the relative cost-effectiveness of its establishment. A principal component multivariate analysis and a Hierarchical Clustering on Principal Components were performed. The economic allocation method assigned a greater environmental burden to meat (83%), followed by energy content (80%) and mass production (73%). The largest sources of GHG emissions were enteric fermentation and manure deposited on pasture. Both cow-calf and fattening systems had a cluster of farms with better productivity, pasture
7: The global population growth combined with increased affluence has led to a quick per capita increase in the supply and consumption of meat and milk, especially in developing countries (de Vries et al., 2015; Roos et al., 2017). This growing demand has led to the rapid growth of the livestock sector, with higher growth rates in the develop- ing countries (Gerber et al., 2013; Thornton and Herrero, 2010), and higher environmental burdens such as GHG emissions, land degrada- tion and deforestation (Cederberg et al., 2011). According to Gerber et al. (2013), the livestock sector is responsible for 14.5% of global green- house gas (GHG) emissions, with beef production accounting for 41% of these emissions (Opio et al., 2013). Therefore, the identification and adoption of environmental-friendly beef production strategies are man- datory to tackle the environmental burden that this sector is generating.

In Latin America, beef cattle production is mostly under open- grazing conditions and is an important social and economic activity (FAO, 2013). The Latin-American countries with the largest beef herds are Brazil, Argentina, Mexico, and Colombia. The beef production chain in Colombia accounts for approximately 11.6 million cattle heads (43% of the national cattle population) and comprises cow-calf, fattening, and full-cycle farms, which annually produce 933 million kg carcass (DANE, 2020). Cow-calf and cattle-fattening farms account for 43 and 39% of the Colombian beef herd, respectively, and the remaining 18% corresponds to full-cycle (DANE, 2020). These farms use traditional-extensive and improved-extensive grazing systems, where animals graze on large plots, under low stocking rates, and receive diets that usually include native forage species, leading to low produc- tivity rates (Barahona et al., 2003; González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c; Mahecha-Ledesma et al., 2002). Another essential production system in the beef value chain is the dual-purpose cattle system, which supplies animals to the cattle fattening systems. In Colombia, forestry and agriculture are responsible for 36 and 26% of national GHG emissions, being by far the productive sectors that emit the most GHG (IDEAM et al., 2016), with emissions from pastures and cattle accounting for 21% of national GHG emissions. Therefore, as the Colombian government committed to reducing 20% of national GHG emissions by 2030 (Gobierno de Colombia, 2015), identifying sustain- able beef production strategies is of paramount importance. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a holistic method to evaluate the envi- ronmental impacts, such as the emission of pollutants (ammonia or methane) and the use of resources (land and fossil fuels), during the en- tire life cycle of a product (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Guinée, 2002). During the last few years, LCA studies have estimated the environmen- tal performance of beef cattle systems in different Latin American coun- tries such as Argentina (Faverin et al., 2019; Modernel et al., 2018), Brazil (Bogaerts et al., 2017; Bustamante et al., 2012; Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2011; Cerri et al., 2016; de Figueiredo et al., 2017; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Florindo et al., 2017a, 2017b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016; Ruviaro et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), Costa Rica (Mazzetto et al., 2020), Mexico (Rivera-Huerta et al., 2016), Paraguay (Costantini et al., 2020), and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Modernel et al., 2013; Picasso et al., 2014). The assessed cattle systems included cow-calf, cattle finishing, cow-calf-to-finishing operations, and other production methods such as confinement and intensive, semi-intensive, and extensive grazing. Most LCA evaluations used modelled farm data and just a few of them included primary data from farms (Bogaerts et al., 2017; Cerri et al.,

2016; Costantini et al., 2020; Mazzetto et al., 2020; Picasso et al., 2014; Ruviaro et al., 2015). There are no LCA studies that have evaluated the environmental performance of the Colombian beef production chain, neither modelling cattle systems nor using data gathered directly from producers. Therefore, this restricts identifying the main hotspots of the environmental impacts of these systems and adequate GHG mitigation strategies to be promoted in public policies or government programs.

The present study had two aims. Firstly, to estimate the environ- mental impact of cow-calf and fattening farms in Colombia by quantify- ing GHG emissions (carbon footprint), non-renewable energy use and land use (LU), using a farm gate LCA approach using data directly gathered from the farms. Secondly, to identify the hotspots of total environmental impacts and opportunities to improve environmental performance and mitigate climate change.

10: We used a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach to assess impact categories such as the carbon footprint (CF or GHG emissions per kg product), non-renewable energy use, and land use in cow-calf and fat- tening cattle systems in Colombia. The LCA was done by the attribu- tional method, which aims to quantify the environmental impact of a system's main co-products in a status quo situation (Thomassen et al., 2008). The publicly available specification PAS, 2050: 2011 - (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) was used based on LCA and allowed the quantifica- tion of GHG emissions in the life cycle of products. Modelling of impact categories was carried out in Microsoft Excel. We estimated carbon foot- prints using global warming potential for a time frame period of 100 years. The global warming potentials for methane, carbon dioxide and nitrous oxide were 28, 1 and 265, respectively (IPCC, 2014).
11: 2.1.1.1. Functional unit and allocation. The functional units used were 1 kg fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) and 1 kg live weight gain (LWG), leaving the farm gate. Cow-calf systems produce meat and milk from dual-purpose COWS (González-Quintero et al., 2020c). To as- sign environmental burdens between milk and meat, we used three dif- ferent allocation methods:

<list>1) An economic allocation method based on the prices of the meat and milk produced in a year. 
2) An energy allocation method based on the energy contents (MJ) of the meat and milk produced in a year. 
3) A mass allocation method based on the mass of meat (LWG) and milk (FPCM) produced in a year.</list>

2.1.1.2. System boundary definition. The system boundary was defined on a "cradle to farm-gate" perspective for cow-calf and fattening farms (Fig. 1). The direct or primary emissions are those generated within the farm system (on-farm), and the secondary off-farm emissions are those upstream emissions related to the production and transport of imported resources such as feed, fertilizer, and soil amendments.

In Fig. 2, the flows in the primary beef production chain in Colombia are shown. Animals entering fattening farms come from either cow-calf or dual-purpose systems. However, the exact precedence of these
CO2 emissions CH4 emissions N20 emissions
Fig. 1. System boundaries and flows accounted for in the estimation of the impact categories in the beef farms in a "cradle to farm-gate" approach.

animals is difficult to track due to the lack of record-keeping by farmers, poor integration between dual-purpose, cow-calf and fattening sys- items, intermediary market actors, animal smuggling, and illegal slaugh- terhouses (Lopez-Ramirez and García-Cáceres, 2020). Usually, when performing an LCA study for fattening farms, calves from other cattle systems are considered an input and the environmental burden of its breeding is accounted for in the receiving system (Mogensen et al., 2015a, 2015b). However, as for the studied cow-calf farms, the calf weaned is the main product; it must pay the full environmental bill for its products except for the co-products such as milk and cull cows sent to slaughter. According to the above, all the environmental costs for raising calves are included in the cow-calf systems (Fig. 2), not as input for fattening farms. Dual-purpose cattle farms are out of the scope of this study.

14: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.
Fig. 3. Model for calculating N balance in the beef cattle systems studied.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
15: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.
Table 1 Mean, 5th, 35th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentile of the characteristics of the 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening Colombian farms evaluated.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.
Fig. 3. Model for calculating N balance in the beef cattle systems studied.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
17: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.
Table 1 Mean, 5th, 35th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentile of the characteristics of the 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening Colombian farms evaluated.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.
Fig. 3. Model for calculating N balance in the beef cattle systems studied.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
20: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.
Table 1 Mean, 5th, 35th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentile of the characteristics of the 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening Colombian farms evaluated.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.
Fig. 3. Model for calculating N balance in the beef cattle systems studied.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
22: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.
Table 1 Mean, 5th, 35th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentile of the characteristics of the 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening Colombian farms evaluated.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.
Fig. 3. Model for calculating N balance in the beef cattle systems studied.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
25: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
27: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.
Table 1 Mean, 5th, 35th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentile of the characteristics of the 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening Colombian farms evaluated.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.4. Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions. Emission factors (EF) used to estimate off-farm GHG emissions from imported feeds and fertilizers are summarized in Table 3. These GHG emissions corresponded to the production and transportation of these respective agricultural inputs.

2.1.2.5. Non-renewable energy use. The amount of energy used for on- farm activities and transportation of inputs from the factory to the farm were calculated according to the Planning Unit of the Mines and Energy of Colombia (UPME, 2016) and the Ecoinvent database (Weidema et al., 2013). Off-farm energy requirements related to agricultural inputs production were estimated using specific factors obtained from Agri- Footprint, Ecoinvent, and European Life Cycle Reference Database (Durlinger et al., 2014; Weidema et al., 2013) as shown in Table 3.

2.1.2.6. Land use. The land use was calculated as the sum of the on-farm grazing area and the off-farm land required to produce the purchased feeds. Grazing areas were obtained during surveys conducted on each farm, and the off-farm land uses were obtained from the Agri- Footprint database and Ecoinvent database, as shown in Table 3.
32: <header>2.1.2. Life Cycle Inventory and impact assessment </header>

2.1.2.1. Farm data. The present study included data collected in surveys carried out in 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening farms located in 13 De- partments in Colombia: Atlántico, Bolívar, Boyacá, Caldas, Cauca, Cesar, La Guajira, Meta, Quindío, Risaralda, Santander, Tolima, and Valle del Cauca. The criteria used to select these farms, the information included in the surveys, the main characteristics of farms, and the data collection process were explicitly described in the characterization

studies of cow-calf and fattening cattle systems performed by González-Quintero et al. (2020b, 2020c). Briefly, the information used in this study was obtained from the Sustainable Colombian Cattle Ranching (GCS, Spanish initials) and the LivestockPlus (L+) projects conducted in Colombia. In these studies, over a period of 1 year, quantitative and qualitative data were collected by applying surveys on farms. Questionnaires used included questions on general information, herd composition and management, pastures manage- ment practices, livestock production and reproduction data, envi- ronmental information, animal health, social information, and financial information. The averages and the 5th, 25th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentiles of variables used to describe the selected farms are presented in Table 1. For cow-calf farms, the amounts of milk produced were corrected for fat (3.7%) and protein (3.3%) content using the method described by Carulla and Ortega (2016). Live weight gain (LWG) was quantified for animals produced on the farms, assuming no change in the on-farm stock size and that no animals were brought into the farm. Gross energy concentration was calculated using IPCC tier 2 equa- tions (Table 2) and considering daily gross energy (GE) intake esti- mated for each animal category based on diet digestibility and daily net energy requirements for maintenance, activity, growth, lactation, and pregnancy (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Dry matter intake (DMI) was computed by dividing herd-specific gross energy intake
Fig. 2. Diagram showing the beef production chain in Colombia.
Table 1 Mean, 5th, 35th, 50th, 75th and 95th percentile of the characteristics of the 251 cow-calf and 275 fattening Colombian farms evaluated.

a FPCM: Fat Protein Corrected Milk (3.5% fat, 3.3% protein). b AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls). C Fertilizer: (N): 8(P): 8(K).

values by the energy density of the feed (18.45) (Gavrilova et al., 2019; IPCC, 2006). Pasture productivity ha yr-1) and nu- trient content and digestibility (%) were estimated based on (i) the re- gion and municipality where the farm is located in the country, (ii) the identification of the main types of pastures for each region by using the atlas of bovine production systems in Colombia (Pulido- Herrera et al., 2005), and (iii) expert informed criteria. Use of fertilizer and lime was expressed as the amount applied over an area (ha) of im- proved pastures.

2.1.2.2. Estimation of on-farm GHG emissions. Calculations of baseline GHG emissions were estimated using 2006 IPCC (IPCC, 2006), and in Chapters 10 (Gavrilova et al., 2019) and 11 (Hergoualc'h et al., 2019) of Volume 4 of the 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines. Equa- tions and emission factors (EF) used to estimate the primary emissions of CH4, N20, and CO2 are summarized in Table 2.

The amount of dry organic matter in manure was determined from the herd-specific gross energy intake, digestibility of feed consumed, default values for ash content in dry matter and CH4 producing capacity, and the methane conversion factor. We did not account for GHG emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks at the farm level because grasslands were established more than 20 years ago, and no deforestation was identified (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Machinery and equipment and infrastructure availability were low or non-existent in most evaluated farms (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c), therefore, GHG emissions from infrastructure were not considered following PAS (BSI and Carbon Trust, 2011) recom- mendations. This was also the case for other LCA studies on Latin American beef systems, which indicated that such emissions were not
included in the evaluation (Cardoso et al., 2016; Cederberg et al., 2009; Dick et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ruviaro et al., 2015).

2.1.2.3. Nitrogen balance at farm level. Nutrient balance is a useful tool for quantifying the flow of nutrients in agricultural systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000). A nitrogen balance at the farm level was made for checking out possible N surplus and thus the risk of N leaching. Fig. 3 shows the model used for estimating N balances. N surplus is defined as the difference between net N-output from the farm in milk and meat and the net N input to the farm (Dalgaard et al., 1998). For each farm, the surplus N was quantified and expressed per unit area (ha) as:

N inputs were estimated by multiplying each input's amount (feeds and fertilizers) by its N content. The N content of fertilizers and feeds were based on the product labels. Annual N deposition (N input) was assumed using a standard estimate (15 kg Nha described by Bobbink et al. (2010). N fixation was set at zero. The N outputs were es- timated by multiplying the amount of milk and live weight produced by their N content. The N content of meat and milk was computed based on their protein content and converted into milk and meat N content, ac- cording to Gavrilova et al. (2019). The N surplus must be transmitted into different emissions, which is performed by applying models for cal- culating N emissions on-farm. N20-N and NH3-N losses were calculated using emission factors from Hergoualc'h et al. (2019) (Table 2).

Scenario analysis was conducted to analyze how technological changes lead to GHG emissions reductions. Based on the characteristics of clusters identified, literature review and expert opinions, the estab- lishment of improved pastures and electric fences for rotational grazing were considered the chosen technologies. Adopting improved pastures was modelled just in the area necessary to produce and satisfy cattle's current forage demand. We assumed the establishment of grass with 65% and 12% of dry matter digestibility and crude protein content, re- spectively (Gutiérrez et al., 2018). Additionally, we estimated the rela- tive cost-effectiveness for establishing and maintaining improved pastures and electric fences, and the quantities of GHG emissions de- creased after implementing these measures. Reductions in GHG emis- sions were estimated as the annual average of the difference between the baseline scenario's total GHG emissions and the total emissions under the mitigation measure scenario (de Oliveira Silva et al., 2015). Cost-effectiveness was estimated from the difference between baseline gross margins and those calculated for each mitigation scenario, then di- viding the result by the reduction in GHG emissions. The gross margins in both mitigation scenarios were estimated as the difference between the revenues and expenses of farms in one year. Revenues come only from the hypothetical sale of all live weight (LW) and all milk produced per farm per year. The milk and meat prices were obtained from the Colombian National Cattle Ranchers Federation (FEDEGAN, 2019). Farm expenses were composed of investment and maintenance costs for the implementation of improved pastures and electric fences. The associated cost for the establishment and maintenance was calculated according to Gutiérrez et al. (2018), including farm operations and quantities of inputs required (e.g., land adaptation, seeding, fertilizers, amendments to the soil, forage seed, and electric fence).
35: Estimation of off-farm GHG emissions, energy use, and land use for cow-calf and fattening farms.
