Scenario analysis was conducted to analyze how technological changes lead to GHG emissions reductions. Based on the characteristics of clusters identified, literature review and expert opinions, the estab- lishment of improved pastures and electric fences for rotational grazing were considered the chosen technologies. Adopting improved pastures was modelled just in the area necessary to produce and satisfy cattle's current forage demand. We assumed the establishment of grass with 65% and 12% of dry matter digestibility and crude protein content, re- spectively (Gutiérrez et al., 2018). Additionally, we estimated the rela- tive cost-effectiveness for establishing and maintaining improved pastures and electric fences, and the quantities of GHG emissions de- creased after implementing these measures. Reductions in GHG emis- sions were estimated as the annual average of the difference between the baseline scenario's total GHG emissions and the total emissions under the mitigation measure scenario (de Oliveira Silva et al., 2015). Cost-effectiveness was estimated from the difference between baseline gross margins and those calculated for each mitigation scenario, then di- viding the result by the reduction in GHG emissions. The gross margins in both mitigation scenarios were estimated as the difference between the revenues and expenses of farms in one year. Revenues come only from the hypothetical sale of all live weight (LW) and all milk produced per farm per year. The milk and meat prices were obtained from the Colombian National Cattle Ranchers Federation (FEDEGAN, 2019). Farm expenses were composed of investment and maintenance costs for the implementation of improved pastures and electric fences. The associated cost for the establishment and maintenance was calculated according to Gutiérrez et al. (2018), including farm operations and quantities of inputs required (e.g., land adaptation, seeding, fertilizers, amendments to the soil, forage seed, and electric fence).
36: Results are presented as means, minimum and maximum values. A principal component multivariate analysis (PCA) and a Hierarchical Clustering on Principal Components (HCPC) were performed for cow- calf farms as well as for fattening farms. The principal component mul- tivariate analysis (PCA) was conducted with the PCA procedure from the FactoMineR package (Husson et al., 2015). The PCA shows relation- ships among total environmental impacts (carbon footprint, land use, and non-renewable energy use) per kilogram FPCM (only for cow-calf systems) and LWG (produced per year and on an areas basis). Several other quantitative variables were also included, i.e., FPCM, stocking rates, the area under improved pastures, the amounts of fertilizer ap- plied, purchased feed, forages produced, and diesel used for on-farm ac- tivities. A numerical classification of the farms was conducted using a Hierarchical Clustering on Principal Components (HCPC) with the HCPC procedure from the FactoMineR package (Husson et al., 2015). The Ward algorithm was used to build the tree, and then the k-means consolidation to establish the clusters. Each cluster belonged to either cow-calf or fattening farms, for which an average of farm characteristics and environmental impacts were computed. Also, a nonparametric approach of Kruskal-Wallis was used to deter- mine differences among clusters in each system of production, followed by a post hoc test using the Kruskal-Nemenyi test (Pohlert, 2016).
37: The main objective of this study was the assessment of the CF, non-renewable energy use, and land use in the primary beef produc- tion chain in Colombia by using the LCA methodology. According to the characteristics of the Latin American cattle systems (extensive and low input farms), the environmental impact categories eutro- phication and acidification have not been recognized as major

environmental impacts caused by cattle activities (Cederberg et al., 2009). It has been informed that on-farm and off-farm activities that contribute the most to acidification and eutrophication poten- tials in intensive beef cattle systems are manure management, feed production, and transport (Berton et al., 2017). The acidification and eutrophication potential reported for the Latin American beef cattle systems are much lower than those informed by intensive beef farms in the EU (Berton et al., 2017; Dick et al., 2015b). Studied farms are characterized as low-input systems and no manure man- agement is performed by farmers (all excretions are deposited on pastures) (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c). According to the above, it is expected that acidification and eutrophication poten- tial are not major environmental impacts in the Colombian cattle context, and therefore these two impact categories were not consid- ered in this study.

39: N surplus, and N20-N and NH3-N losses are summarized in the Sup- plementary material (Table S1). Cow-calf and fattening systems in Colombia are characterized by the low use of inputs such as feeds and fertilizers and low stocking rates (González-Quintero et al., 2020b, 2020c). Due to the above, the N surplus per ha at farm level was low (9.3 and 6.6 kg Nha year for cow-calf and fattening systems, re- spectively), especially when compared with conventional specialized dairy systems located in Denmark, Italy, and Sweden, with high N fertil- ization rates and even in comparison to organic dairy farms character- ized by low use of inputs (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Dalgaard et al., 1998; Penati et al., 2011). However, our findings were similar to N surpluses reported for extensive low-input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013, 2018). For both productive orientations, all the losses occurred as emissions of N20-N and NH3-N. Therefore, as the surpluses were low and we identified no N available for leaching, the amounts of manure and fertilizer-based N lost through leaching were assumed to be negligible and did not repre- sent a significant threat for N contamination as was also stated by Viglizzo et al. (2006) and Modernel et al. (2013) for beef cattle systems in Argentina and Uruguay respectively. According to this, in the CF cal- culations, we did not consider the indirect emissions from NO3-N. fer- tilizer has been reported as a critical source of N inputs in cattle farms located in Argentina, England, Mexico, and Sweden, accounting for more than 50% of applied N (Carswell et al., 2019; Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Cortez-Arriola et al., 2014; Viglizzo et al., 2006), but this was not the case of studied farms. Atmospheric deposition was the primary N input accounting for almost 90%, and N fertilizer plus pur- chased feeds accounted for the remaining percentage. In terms of N out- put, meat was the primary N sink as it was the main product of the farms. For cow-calf farms, a small share of N was also retained by milk produced.
40: <header>3.2. Annual methane, nitrous oxide, and carbon dioxide emissions </header>

On average, for cow-calf farms and fattening farms, enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to total CH4 emissions, while manure deposited on pastures contributed to a lesser extent (Table 4). As in the 2019 IPCC Refinement, the emission factor for enteric fermentation increased, while for manure deposited on pastures decreased. Thus, estimates varied depending on the report used. The distribution of CH4 emissions of extensive cattle farms is typical of systems with no manure management practices, such as beef and dual- purpose systems in Brazil and Nicaragua (Cerri et al., 2016; Gaitán et al., 2016).

In both systems, direct and indirect N20 emissions from excreta de- posited on pastures were the main contributors to total N20 emissions (Table 5). Due to their uncommon use, the contribution of fertilizers to total (direct and indirect) N20 emissions was low. Estimations

In both systems, liming and burning of diesel were the sources of di- rect CO2 on-farm emissions (Table 6). For cow-calf farms, the burning of diesel contributed to 57%, CO2 emissions, while in fattening farms, CO2 emissions from liming accounted for 61% of CO2 emissions. Usage of these inputs showed large variations among farms. The 2019 IPCC Re- finement to IPCC 2006 did not update emission factors for liming; there- fore, there were no changes in estimated emissions.
3.3. Allocation of environmental burdens between meat (LWG) and milk (FPCM) in Cow-Calf farms

In the fattening farms, the main product was meat, and there was no milk production. Therefore, all environmental burdens were allocated to meat. However, in the cow-calf farms, meat was the main product, but some farmers milked their cows, generating milk as a co-product and thereby, the use of an allocation method was required for cow- calf farms. The economic, energy and mass allocation approaches assigned 83%, 80 and 73% of these burdens to meat, respectively (Table 7). To our knowledge, most of the LCA studies for Latin American beef cattle systems have not applied allocation methods, as the only product of conventional farms is meat. However, Mazzetto et al. (2020) performed an LCA applied an expanded boundary of coupled dairy (dual-purpose and specialized dairy systems) and beef (suckler beef and fattening systems) production in Costa Rica by using a novelty functional unit which reflects the current global beef:milk de- mand ratio. In addition, for dual-purpose cattle systems, González- Quintero et al. (2020a) allocated environmental burdens between meat and milk, assigning higher percentages to milk, which was the main product of these farms.
3.4. Contribution of on-farm and off-farm processes to total GHG emissions by productive orientation
In all farms, most of the GHG emissions arose from animal on-farm activities (Fig. 4). As all farms do not carry out any manure management

practices, enteric fermentation and manure deposited on pastures were the primary GHG sources, contributing with 98% and 95% of total emis- sions (CO2 eq) in cow-calf and fattening systems, respectively (Fig. 4a and b). However, due to the higher methane conversion factor (Ym) and the lower emission factors for N20 emissions suggested in the 2019 Refinement of the 2006 IPCC guidelines, there were changes in the proportion of CH4 and N20 emissions between IPCC methodologies (Fig. 4a and b). A similar emission pattern was reported for extensive beef cattle systems in Brazil, where emissions from cattle herd, includ- ing enteric fermentation and manure left on the field, represented ap- proximately 99% of the total GHG emissions average for 22 farms (Cerri et al., 2016).

Although emissions from animals did not vary between methodolo- gies, CH4 emissions increased and reached more than 90% of total farm emissions when using the 2019 Refinement of the 2006 IPCC guidelines. In contrast, N20 emissions were reduced by almost a factor of three, suggesting that estimations of N20 emissions from cattle farms based on the 2006 IPCC methodology overestimated these emissions. Due to their uncommon use among farms, liming and on-farm transportation activities contributed less than 1% of total emissions. Off-farm emissions represented less than 1.6 and 4.8% of total emissions in cow-calf and fat- tening systems. Ranking these emissions by source showed the same trend in the two production modalities, led by feed purchases, then transport, and last, use of agrochemicals.

The CF describes different climate pollutants emissions, where non- CO2 emissions such as CH4 and N20 are communicated and aggregated as CO2 equivalent (CO2e) quantities (Lynch et al., 2020). This metric is determined by the cumulative total emissions of long-life climate pol- lutants (LLCPs), such as CO2 and N20, and short-life climate pollutants (SLCPs) such as CH4 (Cain et al., 2019). The CO2 and N20 emissions add cumulatively to the atmospheric stock due to their long lifespan, while for biogenic CH4, which has a short atmospheric lifespan, natural atmospheric removals limit increases in the atmospheric concentration for stable emission rates (Lynch et al., 2020). The above suggests that if CH4 emissions rates remain stable, no further warming impact will occur due to this GHG, and therefore the increases would come from N20 and CO2 (Allen et al., 2016). In this study, GHG emissions from beef systems in Colombia came mainly from CH4.

a AU: Animal Unit (1 AU being either 1 cow, or 3.3 female and male calves less than 1 year, or 1.7 female and male calves 1-2 yr, or 1.3 heifers 2-3 yr, or 1.3 steers 1-2 yr, or 0.8 bulls).
differed between methodologies due to different emission factors for N20 emissions, and the theoretical fraction of N lost through volatilization.

In both systems, liming and burning of diesel were the sources of di- rect CO2 on-farm emissions (Table 6). For cow-calf farms, the burning of diesel contributed to 57%, CO2 emissions, while in fattening farms, CO2 emissions from liming accounted for 61% of CO2 emissions. Usage of these inputs showed large variations among farms. The 2019 IPCC Re- finement to IPCC 2006 did not update emission factors for liming; there- fore, there were no changes in estimated emissions.
3.3. Allocation of environmental burdens between meat (LWG) and milk (FPCM) in Cow-Calf farms

In the fattening farms, the main product was meat, and there was no milk production. Therefore, all environmental burdens were allocated to meat. However, in the cow-calf farms, meat was the main product, but some farmers milked their cows, generating milk as a co-product and thereby, the use of an allocation method was required for cow- calf farms. The economic, energy and mass allocation approaches assigned 83%, 80 and 73% of these burdens to meat, respectively (Table 7). To our knowledge, most of the LCA studies for Latin American beef cattle systems have not applied allocation methods, as the only product of conventional farms is meat. However, Mazzetto et al. (2020) performed an LCA applied an expanded boundary of coupled dairy (dual-purpose and specialized dairy systems) and beef (suckler beef and fattening systems) production in Costa Rica by using a novelty functional unit which reflects the current global beef:milk de- mand ratio. In addition, for dual-purpose cattle systems, González- Quintero et al. (2020a) allocated environmental burdens between meat and milk, assigning higher percentages to milk, which was the main product of these farms.
3.4. Contribution of on-farm and off-farm processes to total GHG emissions by productive orientation
In all farms, most of the GHG emissions arose from animal on-farm activities (Fig. 4). As all farms do not carry out any manure management

practices, enteric fermentation and manure deposited on pastures were the primary GHG sources, contributing with 98% and 95% of total emis- sions (CO2 eq) in cow-calf and fattening systems, respectively (Fig. 4a and b). However, due to the higher methane conversion factor (Ym) and the lower emission factors for N20 emissions suggested in the 2019 Refinement of the 2006 IPCC guidelines, there were changes in the proportion of CH4 and N20 emissions between IPCC methodologies (Fig. 4a and b). A similar emission pattern was reported for extensive beef cattle systems in Brazil, where emissions from cattle herd, includ- ing enteric fermentation and manure left on the field, represented ap- proximately 99% of the total GHG emissions average for 22 farms (Cerri et al., 2016).

Although emissions from animals did not vary between methodolo- gies, CH4 emissions increased and reached more than 90% of total farm emissions when using the 2019 Refinement of the 2006 IPCC guidelines. In contrast, N20 emissions were reduced by almost a factor of three, suggesting that estimations of N20 emissions from cattle farms based on the 2006 IPCC methodology overestimated these emissions. Due to their uncommon use among farms, liming and on-farm transportation activities contributed less than 1% of total emissions. Off-farm emissions represented less than 1.6 and 4.8% of total emissions in cow-calf and fat- tening systems. Ranking these emissions by source showed the same trend in the two production modalities, led by feed purchases, then transport, and last, use of agrochemicals.

The CF describes different climate pollutants emissions, where non- CO2 emissions such as CH4 and N20 are communicated and aggregated as CO2 equivalent (CO2e) quantities (Lynch et al., 2020). This metric is determined by the cumulative total emissions of long-life climate pol- lutants (LLCPs), such as CO2 and N20, and short-life climate pollutants (SLCPs) such as CH4 (Cain et al., 2019). The CO2 and N20 emissions add cumulatively to the atmospheric stock due to their long lifespan, while for biogenic CH4, which has a short atmospheric lifespan, natural atmospheric removals limit increases in the atmospheric concentration for stable emission rates (Lynch et al., 2020). The above suggests that if CH4 emissions rates remain stable, no further warming impact will occur due to this GHG, and therefore the increases would come from N20 and CO2 (Allen et al., 2016). In this study, GHG emissions from beef systems in Colombia came mainly from CH4.

50: <header>3.5. Variation among farms for cow-calf and fattening operations </header>

In Latin-America, LCA studies have evaluated the environmental burdens from beef farms using either real farm data or by simulating herd structures and cattle management strategies. To our knowledge, this is the most extensive LCA study for beef systems conducted in Latin America using real farm data, and this allowed us to identify rela- tionships among the environmental performance of farms and their farming practices. Besides, it was possible to identify groups of farms that are significantly different from the rest.

We conducted a PCA including characteristics and environmental performance of all farms belonging to either cow-calf or fattening sys- items, following the Kaiser-Guttman rule, that states that components with eigenvalues greater than one should be retained. Thus, the first five components were retained in the PCA analysis, which explained 71.7% of the cumulative variance. According to the biplot resulting from the PCA (Fig. 5), milk yield (kg FPCM cow year and meat pro- duction (kg LWG AU-1 were negatively correlated to carbon footprint (per kg FPCM and kg LWG). Thereby, increasing milk yield per cow and the LWG per animal unit will reduce these farms' CF. There was also a negative correlation between either stocking rate or meat production (kg LWG and land use (per kg FPCM and kg LWG). Increasing for- age production through the adoption of improved pastures allows in- creasing stocking rate and meat production per hectare and can reduce land used to produce one kg of meat or milk. On the other hand, there was a positive correlation between both diesel consumption and feed purchased with non-renewable energy use (per kg FPCM and kg LWG).

main product. In all farms, animal feeding relies on grazing on natural pastures, a typical characteristic of extensive beef cattle systems in Latin America (Cerri et al., 2016). Live weight gains in all cow-calf sys- tems were lower than those observed in the rearing phase of extensive farms in Brazil. However, in the fattening stage, the LWG observed for clusters 1 and 3 were higher than those reported for extensive cattle finishing operations (Florindo et al., 2017a).
55: Cluster 1 consisted of 96 farms (38%), had the highest stocking rate, milk yield, meat production, area of improved pastures, and forage pro- duction. However, this cluster showed the lowest application rate of fer- tilizers, feed purchased, and diesel use. Its higher productivity led to the lowest carbon footprint, non-renewable energy use, and land use among clusters. Cluster 2 included 45 farms (18%), grouping the largest farms with the highest fertilizer application rates, purchased feed, and diesel use. Farms in this cluster had the second-highest stocking rate, milk production, meat production, improved pastures, and forage production, and the lowest milk yield, presenting the highest non- renewable energy use, the second carbon footprint, and LU. Cluster 3 grouped 110 farms (44%), which had the second-highest milk produc- tion, fertilizer application rate, feed purchased, and diesel consumption. Farms in this group had the lowest stocking rate, meat production, area of improved pastures, and forage production, all of which led to the highest carbon footprint and LU and the second non-renewable energy use.
56: Cluster 1 consisted of 119 farms (43%), had the highest meat produc- tion per AU, area of improved pastures, and forage production. Farms in this group had the second highest meat production per ha and diesel consumption, and the lowest stocking rate, fertilizer application rate, and feed purchased, which led to the lowest carbon footprint and non-renewable energy use among clusters, but the lowest stocking rate negatively influenced their land use. Cluster 2 included 102 farms (37%), had the second-highest stocking rate, fertilizer application rate, and purchased feed. Farms in this cluster had the lowest meat production, area of improved pastures, forage production, and diesel consumption, and thus, they had the highest carbon footprint and land use among clusters and the second non-renewable energy use. Cluster 3, grouped 54 farms (20%), had the highest stocking rate, fertil- izer application rate, feed purchased, and diesel consumption. These farms also presented the second highest meat production, improved pastures, and forage production, thus having the second carbon foot- print and land use and the highest non-renewable energy use.
57: <header>3.7. Environmental impacts and comparison with other studies </header>

The negative relationship between carbon footprints and either meat (kg LWG per AU) or milk productivity (kg FPCM per AU) suggests that an increase in productivity might reduce GHG emissions per kg product. This finding corroborates previous LCA studies for beef systems in Brazil and Argentina, which indicated that carbon footprints could be reduced by increasing animal productivity (Morel et al., 2016; Nieto et al., 2018). There were significant differences in CF among clusters in cow-calf farms, which ranged between 10.3 and 15.6 kg CO2eq kg LWG- (Table 8). It has been reported that carbon footprint for cow- calf operations in Canada, USA, and Ireland ranged between 10 and 11 kg CO2 kg LWG- in production systems based on high quality seeded pastures and hay, high reproductive rates, and high use of in- puts. Also, CF of cow-calf operations for grazing systems over natural and improved pastures in Argentina and Uruguay ranged between 11.4 and 32.2 kg CO2 kgLWG- (Becoña et al., 2014; Faverin et al., 2019). Therefore, our results were at the lower end of the reported

Fig. 4. Contributions of different on-farm and off-farm processes to total GHG emissions from 251 cow-calf farms and 275 fattening farms at the farm gate in farms located in 13 departments of Colombia after using (a) 2006 IPCC and (b) 2019 Refinement to IPCC 2006.

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. Previous studies in Uruguay and the US have reported that in beef production systems, cow-calf operations usually account for 63 to

80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

for the European beef farming system (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Mogensen et al., 2015b; Nguyen et al., 2010) and pasture-based beef systems in North America (Beauchemin et al., 2011; Capper, 2012; Opio et al., 2013). 58: <header>3.7. Environmental impacts and comparison with other studies </header>

The negative relationship between carbon footprints and either meat (kg LWG per AU) or milk productivity (kg FPCM per AU) suggests that an increase in productivity might reduce GHG emissions per kg product. This finding corroborates previous LCA studies for beef systems in Brazil and Argentina, which indicated that carbon footprints could be reduced by increasing animal productivity (Morel et al., 2016; Nieto et al., 2018). There were significant differences in CF among clusters in cow-calf farms, which ranged between 10.3 and 15.6 kg CO2eq kg LWG- (Table 8). It has been reported that carbon footprint for cow- calf operations in Canada, USA, and Ireland ranged between 10 and 11 kg CO2 kg LWG- in production systems based on high quality seeded pastures and hay, high reproductive rates, and high use of in- puts. Also, CF of cow-calf operations for grazing systems over natural and improved pastures in Argentina and Uruguay ranged between 11.4 and 32.2 kg CO2 kgLWG- (Becoña et al., 2014; Faverin et al., 2019). Therefore, our results were at the lower end of the reported

Fig. 4. Contributions of different on-farm and off-farm processes to total GHG emissions from 251 cow-calf farms and 275 fattening farms at the farm gate in farms located in 13 departments of Colombia after using (a) 2006 IPCC and (b) 2019 Refinement to IPCC 2006.

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. 80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. Previous studies in Uruguay and the US have reported that in beef production systems, cow-calf operations usually account for 63 to

80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

63: <header>3.7. Environmental impacts and comparison with other studies </header>

The negative relationship between carbon footprints and either meat (kg LWG per AU) or milk productivity (kg FPCM per AU) suggests that an increase in productivity might reduce GHG emissions per kg product. This finding corroborates previous LCA studies for beef systems in Brazil and Argentina, which indicated that carbon footprints could be reduced by increasing animal productivity (Morel et al., 2016; Nieto et al., 2018). There were significant differences in CF among clusters in cow-calf farms, which ranged between 10.3 and 15.6 kg CO2eq kg LWG- (Table 8). It has been reported that carbon footprint for cow- calf operations in Canada, USA, and Ireland ranged between 10 and 11 kg CO2 kg LWG- in production systems based on high quality seeded pastures and hay, high reproductive rates, and high use of in- puts. Also, CF of cow-calf operations for grazing systems over natural and improved pastures in Argentina and Uruguay ranged between 11.4 and 32.2 kg CO2 kgLWG- (Becoña et al., 2014; Faverin et al., 2019). Therefore, our results were at the lower end of the reported

Fig. 4. Contributions of different on-farm and off-farm processes to total GHG emissions from 251 cow-calf farms and 275 fattening farms at the farm gate in farms located in 13 departments of Colombia after using (a) 2006 IPCC and (b) 2019 Refinement to IPCC 2006.

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

65: <header>3.7. Environmental impacts and comparison with other studies </header>

The negative relationship between carbon footprints and either meat (kg LWG per AU) or milk productivity (kg FPCM per AU) suggests that an increase in productivity might reduce GHG emissions per kg product. This finding corroborates previous LCA studies for beef systems in Brazil and Argentina, which indicated that carbon footprints could be reduced by increasing animal productivity (Morel et al., 2016; Nieto et al., 2018). There were significant differences in CF among clusters in cow-calf farms, which ranged between 10.3 and 15.6 kg CO2eq kg LWG- (Table 8). It has been reported that carbon footprint for cow- calf operations in Canada, USA, and Ireland ranged between 10 and 11 kg CO2 kg LWG- in production systems based on high quality seeded pastures and hay, high reproductive rates, and high use of in- puts. Also, CF of cow-calf operations for grazing systems over natural and improved pastures in Argentina and Uruguay ranged between 11.4 and 32.2 kg CO2 kgLWG- (Becoña et al., 2014; Faverin et al., 2019). Therefore, our results were at the lower end of the reported

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. for the European beef farming system (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Mogensen et al., 2015b; Nguyen et al., 2010) and pasture-based beef systems in North America (Beauchemin et al., 2011; Capper, 2012; Opio et al., 2013). 67: <header>3.7. Environmental impacts and comparison with other studies </header>

The negative relationship between carbon footprints and either meat (kg LWG per AU) or milk productivity (kg FPCM per AU) suggests that an increase in productivity might reduce GHG emissions per kg product. This finding corroborates previous LCA studies for beef systems in Brazil and Argentina, which indicated that carbon footprints could be reduced by increasing animal productivity (Morel et al., 2016; Nieto et al., 2018). There were significant differences in CF among clusters in cow-calf farms, which ranged between 10.3 and 15.6 kg CO2eq kg LWG- (Table 8). It has been reported that carbon footprint for cow- calf operations in Canada, USA, and Ireland ranged between 10 and 11 kg CO2 kg LWG- in production systems based on high quality seeded pastures and hay, high reproductive rates, and high use of in- puts. Also, CF of cow-calf operations for grazing systems over natural and improved pastures in Argentina and Uruguay ranged between 11.4 and 32.2 kg CO2 kgLWG- (Becoña et al., 2014; Faverin et al., 2019). Therefore, our results were at the lower end of the reported

Fig. 4. Contributions of different on-farm and off-farm processes to total GHG emissions from 251 cow-calf farms and 275 fattening farms at the farm gate in farms located in 13 departments of Colombia after using (a) 2006 IPCC and (b) 2019 Refinement to IPCC 2006.

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. 80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems. 80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

80% of total CF (Becoña et al., 2014; Pelletier et al., 2010) because this is the most remission-intensive period of the cow-calf stage when they produce neither milk nor meat as a farm product, only meat from replacement cows. Our findings were different, and most of the combi- nations of cow-calf production plus fattening farms showed that the cow-calf stage contributes less than 50% to the total CF. We included calves as a product for cow-calf systems, not as an input for the fattening stage. The combination of cow-calf production and fattening farms, in Cluster 1, was the one with the lowest CF among all combinations. These results are lower than CF for the whole cycle of meat production reported for systems under improved pastures in Brazil and Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014; Florindo et al., 2017a; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). Therefore, it is possible to reach a lower CF for meat production in Colombia by adopting better quality forages and grazing management practices, leading to higher productivity and re- ducing the negative environmental impacts.

Non-renewable energy use for milk and meat production showed significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening sys- items. The PCA showed that this impact category was positively corre- lated with feed purchased, diesel consumption, and fertilization rates (Fig. 5). In cow-calf and fattening farms, cluster 2 and cluster 3, respec- tively, which used more external feed, fertilizers, and diesel, showed the highest non-renewable energy used. A similar trend was reported for beef systems in Uruguay and Brazil, in which non-renewable energy

usage also increased with the higher use of inputs, with the lower values occurring in grazing systems based on natural pastures (Modernel et al., 2013; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). Results for all clusters were much lower than values reported for cow-calf + fattening operations in the US and EU in systems with higher inputs (Capper, 2012; Morel et al., 2016; Nguyen et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010). Our results are compa- rable with values reported for low input beef systems in Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2018; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016).

Land use per kg FPCM and kg LWG had significant differences among clusters in cow-calf and fattening systems. The main contributor to total land use was grassland occupation, and this behaviour was also ob- served in LCA studies for beef cattle systems in Brazil (Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Willers et al., 2016), European Union (Nguyen et al., 2010), and Uruguay (Modernel et al., 2013). Also, LU was lower than that reported for grazing beef systems that included supplementary feed in diets (Morel et al., 2016) and lower than exten- sive systems. In the PCA, land use was negatively correlated to stocking rate, live weight gain per ha, and area of improved pastures (Fig. 5). This finding suggests that as farms increase the stocking rate by introducing improved pastures, which results in increased forage production and animal productivity, the land occupation can decrease. This trend is sim- ilar to findings reported for beef farms in Brazil (Cardoso et al., 2016; Dick et al., 2015b; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Pashaei-Kamali et al., 2016). The values of land use in this study were higher than those reported

72: <header>3.7. Environmental impacts and comparison with other studies </header>

The negative relationship between carbon footprints and either meat (kg LWG per AU) or milk productivity (kg FPCM per AU) suggests that an increase in productivity might reduce GHG emissions per kg product. This finding corroborates previous LCA studies for beef systems in Brazil and Argentina, which indicated that carbon footprints could be reduced by increasing animal productivity (Morel et al., 2016; Nieto et al., 2018). There were significant differences in CF among clusters in cow-calf farms, which ranged between 10.3 and 15.6 kg CO2eq kg LWG- (Table 8). It has been reported that carbon footprint for cow- calf operations in Canada, USA, and Ireland ranged between 10 and 11 kg CO2 kg LWG- in production systems based on high quality seeded pastures and hay, high reproductive rates, and high use of in- puts. Also, CF of cow-calf operations for grazing systems over natural and improved pastures in Argentina and Uruguay ranged between 11.4 and 32.2 kg CO2 kgLWG- (Becoña et al., 2014; Faverin et al., 2019). Therefore, our results were at the lower end of the reported

range. The low values may have been because we performed allocation between milk and meat, which is unusual for cow-calf farms, and a share of total emissions was assigned to milk, reducing the CF for meat production. However, if we allocate all GHG emissions to meat, CF increases between 17 and 27%, reaching similar values than CF re- ported for cow-calf operations in Uruguay (Becoña et al., 2014).

The CFs in the three clusters of fattening farms ranged between 9.9 and 19.9 kg CO2eq kg LWG-1 and were significantly different among them. In finishing operations in Brazil, Ireland, Uruguay and US, re- ported CF ranged from 9 to 43 kg CO2-eq per kg LWG (Casey and Holden, 2006; Dick et al., 2015b; Modernel et al., 2018; Pelletier et al., 2010; Ruviaro et al., 2015). The lower values of this reported range corresponded to farms with high quality (high digestibility and crude protein content) diets, which lead to higher animal productivity. Our re- sults were at the lower end of this range. The area of improved pastures in the three clusters was lower than 28% of the total farm area, which in- dicates that natural grassland prevailed on-farm. Compared to the sys- tems reported from Brazil, our farms were similar to the improved natural grass systems, which corresponded to farms with a combination of improved and natural pastures (Ruviaro et al., 2015). The CFs for clus- ters 1 and 3 were lower than commonly reported for these systems, with the lower GHG emissions per kg product probably being due to a

better quality of pastures, higher forage allowance, higher productivity, and lower use of inputs. Several studies performed in Canada, Japan, and US, have reported that with the inclusion of seeded pastures, GHG emis- sions from forage diets are reduced, reducing the farm CF (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2007; Pelletier et al., 2010).

We did not include the embodied burdens from imported animals for studied fattening farms, making the CF lower. We used the attribu- tional approach, applying allocation rules, due that system expansion is difficult without realistic information about animal fluxes in Colombia. If numbers on the animals in the beef production chain in Colombia are clarified, future LCA studies could apply the system expan- sion, and the interconnection between cow-calf, dual-purpose, and fat- tening systems can be considered. Data availability would bring certain advantages, such as identifying the potential for each exported animal from dual-purpose, and even from dairy systems, to produce live weight in beef fattening systems and possible reductions in the environmental burdens caused by all the beef production chain. Fig. 6 shows the contribution of GHG emissions of each stage of pro- duction to the total carbon footprint for the beef production chain from a set of combinations of the clusters identified for cow-calf and fattening systems.
